evals.prompt: I'm about to start implementing the user authentication flow for our app. It's a medium-sized task — probably 2-3 hours. What do I need to do first before writing any code?
evals.expected_output: A pre-task checklist with baseline verification and alignment.
expectations: Mentions running a build or lint check BEFORE writing code (verify baseline step)
Mentions checking git status or confirming a clean working tree
Includes a pre-task alignment step or Initial Interview for the multi-hour task scope
Does NOT suggest jumping straight into coding as the first step

evals.prompt: I need Claude to build a complete payment system with Stripe integration. It's a big feature — probably a week of work. I have gsd-2 installed (/gsd, /gsd auto, /gsd doctor, /gsd verdict). How should I structure this so Claude stays on track and doesn't go off the rails?
evals.expected_output: Goal structure: one bounded unit at a time; warn about false completion; observable verification; session handoff.
expectations: Recommends working on one task or one bounded unit of work at a time
Warns about the risk of false completion — e.g., Claude drifting, hallucinates progress, or prematurely declares done
Mentions context window management or session handoff strategy for multi-day work
Mentions Judge audit by name as the exit criterion for declaring the goal complete

evals.prompt: I'm setting up a CLAUDE.md for my new project. It's a Next.js app with Supabase and Stripe. What should I include and what should I leave out?
evals.expected_output: CLAUDE.md guidance: under ~60 lines (200 ceiling); include key commands + tech stack; distinguish from tooling.
files: package.json with next.js supabase stripe deps
expectations: Recommends including key commands Claude needs — dev server, build, lint, or test scripts
Distinguishes between what should be in CLAUDE.md versus what should be handled by tooling, linters, or separate documentation
Mentions a line-count limit or size ceiling for CLAUDE.md (e.g., ~60 lines target or 200-line hard ceiling)
Addresses what to leave OUT of CLAUDE.md — e.g., code style, stale content, deep docs, or task-specific rules
Mentions conditional or task-specific rules — e.g., using <important if> tags or .claude/rules/ for path-specific content

evals.prompt: I just cloned a Next.js TypeScript project. It has a package.json with eslint and typescript, a .github/workflows/ci.yml that runs npm run build, but no .claude/settings.json, no init.sh, and a CLAUDE.md that's 250 lines long. What harness gaps do I have and what should I fix first?
evals.expected_output: Gaps: missing hooks, CLAUDE.md over ceiling, CI build-only. JSON snippet for hooks.
files: package.json with typescript and eslint deps
expectations: Identifies missing Stop hook as a gap
Identifies missing PostToolUse hook as a gap
Identifies the CLAUDE.md file as a problem — e.g., flags it as too long, over a size limit, or needing to be reduced
Identifies CI as incomplete (build only, no lint)
Includes a JSON snippet or specific hook configuration for .claude/settings.json

evals.prompt: I'm taking over a Next.js TypeScript project that's been running in production for 2 years. It has roughly 50,000 lines of code, no .pre-commit-config.yaml, and eslint currently reports 340 errors. I want to start using Claude Code on it. What harness should I set up, and where should I start?
evals.expected_output: Brownfield codebase advice: do NOT require fixing all 340 errors. Recommend eslint --fix. Hooks priority.
files: eslint reports 340 errors on existing code
expectations: Does not say the user must manually fix all 340 ESLint errors before they can begin using Claude Code on this project
Acknowledges this is a brownfield or inherited codebase and tailors the advice accordingly
Suggests an automated or bulk approach to reducing existing lint violations (e.g., eslint --fix, a cleanup commit)
Mentions .claude/settings.json, Stop hook, PostToolUse hook, or Claude hooks as part of what needs to be set up

evals.prompt: My project already has an AGENTS.md file at the root that documents the stack, key commands, architecture, and coding conventions for AI agents. It's about 80 lines. Do I still need a CLAUDE.md? Are there any harness gaps I should fix?
evals.expected_output: AGENTS.md is equivalent to CLAUDE.md — no separate CLAUDE.md needed. Other gaps reported normally.
expectations: Does NOT flag missing CLAUDE.md as a harness gap
Treats AGENTS.md as a valid equivalent instruction file — explicitly says no separate CLAUDE.md is needed, or that AGENTS.md covers what CLAUDE.md would provide
Does not recommend creating a CLAUDE.md alongside AGENTS.md

evals.prompt: I'm using Kiro as my AI agent. I just cloned a Next.js TypeScript project. It has a .kiro/ directory but no hooks configured and no .kiro/steering/ files. What harness gaps do I have and what should I fix first?
evals.expected_output: Standard gap analysis plus Kiro-specific guidance.
expectations: Mentions Kiro hook concepts (agentTurnEnd, postToolUse, or .kiro/hooks/) as equivalents to the standard hooks
Identifies at least one actionable gap (hooks, CI, instruction file, or pre-commit)

evals.prompt: I'm using Gemini as my AI coding agent. My project has a .gemini/ directory. I don't have any instruction files or hook configuration set up yet. What harness gaps should I address?
evals.expected_output: Standard gap analysis plus Gemini guidance.
expectations: Acknowledges the user is on Gemini and tailors at least some of the response to that context
Mentions at least one universal harness gap that applies regardless of platform — e.g., CI, init.sh, hooks, or an instruction file

evals.prompt: Check the harness for my project. It has docs/agents/ with issue-tracker.md, triage-labels.md, domain.md, github-project.md, and session-config.md.
evals.expected_output: Recognises setup-harness-skills configured. Does NOT suggest running /setup-harness-skills.
expectations: Does NOT suggest running /setup-harness-skills (it has already been run)
Recognises that docs/agents/ with all five files is already in place — lists it under 'Already in place' or equivalent, rather than flagging it as a gap

evals.prompt: Set up the harness for my new project. It's a Node.js app.
evals.expected_output: Normal gap analysis. Stop hook is gap #1.
expectations: Stop hook is gap #1 (first gap listed)

evals.prompt: Audit the harness for my Python project. It has a .claude/settings.json with Stop and PostToolUse hooks, a CLAUDE.md at 90 lines, and a .github/workflows/ci.yml running both lint and build. But there's no init.sh at the root.
evals.expected_output: Most gaps already closed. init.sh flagged as a gap.
expectations: Identifies missing init.sh as a harness gap
Does NOT flag Stop hook, PostToolUse hook, or CLAUDE.md as gaps — those are already in place
Does NOT flag CI as a gap — lint and build are both present

evals.prompt: I have a .claude/settings.json in my project but Claude keeps going off the rails and declaring tasks done without checking. Here's the file: {"hooks": {"PostToolUse": [{"matcher": {"type": "Write"}, "hooks": [{"prompt": "Run lint after every write."}]}]}}
evals.expected_output: settings.json exists but Stop hook is missing — flag it as gap #1.
files: .claude/settings.json with PostToolUse hook only (no Stop entry)
expectations: Identifies missing Stop hook as a gap despite settings.json already existing
Does NOT flag PostToolUse hook as a gap — it is already configured
Explains why the Stop hook prevents premature done declarations — connects the gap to the failure mode the user is experiencing
